mnemonics.title: The Interrupt Service Routine Journey
mnemonics.content: I.C.S.S.R.R. = Interrupt Comes, Save state, Service interrupt, Restore state, Return to program. Think: 'I Can Serve, So Return Right' - The CPU stops main work (Interrupt), Saves what it was doing, Services the urgent request, Restores the saved work, Returns happily!

mnemonics.title: Three Types of Interrupts
mnemonics.content: H.I.T. = Hardware, Internal (Software), Timer. Remember: 'Hardware Interrupts Tell' - External devices (keyboard, mouse), Software/Internal (traps, exceptions), Timer (system clock)

mnemonics.title: Interrupt Priority Levels
mnemonics.content: P.I.E. = Priority Interrupt Encoder. Remember: 'Priority Interrupts Encode' - Higher priority devices get CPU attention first through priority encoder circuits

mnemonics.title: Vector Table Memory
mnemonics.content: V.I.P. = Vector Interrupt Points. Remember: 'Very Important Points' - Each interrupt type has a specific memory address (vector) where the ISR address is stored

mnemonics.title: Daisy Chain Priority
mnemonics.content: D.C.P. = Daisy Chain Priority. Remember: 'Don't Cut Power' - Devices are connected in series; priority is determined by position in the chain

acronyms.usage: Signal sent to CPU by I/O device requesting attention. Use when: Discussing the initial interrupt signal from peripheral to processor
Specific program code that handles the interrupt. Use when: Referring to the software routine that services/handles the interrupt
Table containing addresses of all ISR locations. Use when: Describing how CPU locates the correct handler for each interrupt type
Register storing address of next instruction. Use when: Explaining how CPU remembers where to return after interrupt handling
Contains CPU flags and status information. Use when: Describing what context information must be saved during interrupts
Assembly instruction generating software interrupt. Use when: Discussing program-initiated interrupts (system calls)
Allows I/O devices direct memory access. Use when: Understanding how some interrupts reduce CPU involvement in data transfer
Chip managing interrupt requests. Use when: Explaining hardware that handles multiple interrupt sources and priority

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a teacher (CPU) grading papers (executing main program). A student raises hand (Interrupt Request). Teacher pauses, marks place in current paper (Save PC/PSW), addresses student (Execute ISR), returns to exact marked place (Restore PC/PSW), continues grading (Resume main program). Draw a circular flow with the main program as outer circle and interrupt handling as inner loop.

Picture a hotel directory (Vector Table). Each room number (interrupt type) has a key card (address of ISR) inside. When interrupt #3 happens, CPU looks at room 3, grabs the key card, and goes to that room (jumps to ISR address).

Think of Russian nesting dolls. A small doll (low priority interrupt) is inside a medium doll (medium priority), inside a big doll (high priority). Higher priority interrupts can open/contain lower ones - but only after finishing with the innermost current task.

Polling = Security guard checking every room one by one 'Is there a problem?' (wastes time). Interrupt = Fire alarm going off - rooms immediately signal when they need attention (efficient). Draw a timeline comparing CPU usage for both methods.

Imagine you're reading a book (main program) and phone rings (interrupt). You use a bookmark (save PC) and note your mood on a sticky note (save PSW), then take the call (execute ISR). After call, you use bookmark to return to exact page and check sticky note to recall where you were - exactly like CPU restoring context.

analogies.analogy: Like a patient pressing the call button in a hospital. The nurse (CPU) is doing rounds (executing main program), but when button is pressed, she must attend to that patient immediately - that's the interrupt request.
Like the emergency response team arriving at an accident. They have specific trained procedures (ISR) to handle the emergency situation efficiently before normal traffic (main program) resumes.

In a restaurant, the fire alarm (highest priority) stops everything, a customer complaint (medium) is addressed before refilling water (low priority). The head chef (CPU) decides what gets attention first based on urgency.
Maskable = Your phone on silent - you can choose to ignore calls. Non-Maskable = Your smoke detector - you cannot ignore it even if you try, it's physically wired to alert you regardless of settings.

Like ambulance response time - the delay between someone calling for help (interrupt request) and the ambulance arriving to help (CPU starting ISR). Lower latency = faster response = better system performance.

Think of a relay race. The baton (attention) is passed down the line of runners (devices). The first runner has highest priority to pass to second, second to third, etc. If first runner doesn't pass, others wait - similar to daisy chain interrupt priority.

Like finishing a phone call and returning to your movie. You pause exactly where you were (saved PC), resume watching from that exact point (restore PC), and remember your emotional state from before (restore PSW). Seamless continuation!
